John and I have known each other for a very, very long time. We go back to meeting at Gary Halbert seminars. If you are not familiar with Gary, then you are negligent and deficient in your duty and responsibility as a marketer. I'm sure you can spell Halbert. I would point out to you, one of the places his legacy lives on. I'm sure you've seen it. John, when's the last time you were at Disney World? Ten years ago. Do they still let you in there now that you got that child predator thing? Yeah, right. Parole? Yeah. So, at Disney, the old Halbert family crest business lives on, but it's now all computerized and it's done instantly on the spot. The next time you're at Disney World and go to Epcot and go to the World Showcase and go to England, and in England you will find the original Halbert business and its newest incarnation living merrily on and still making tons and tons and tons of money. So John and I go back to some very interesting, is there anybody in here who was at the $7,000 per person Gary Halbert seminars? Is there anybody? A couple. There you go. Yeah, a few. So a very eclectic cast. Chase Ravel, the creator of Entrepreneur Magazine, who is a story in and of himself. So we have all these stories that we can't tell till we outlive everybody about whom we wish to tell the stories. And then maybe a little while out of respect for the, some respect for the dead. But John and I go back a very long time, mutual respect society. We both basically approach selling and copywriting with the same sort of killer take all attitude. We view it as a blood sport. We like it. We like the thrill of the kill. We believe it is an honorable thing to do, assuming you are selling something honorable. And no one is more adept at what John is going to talk to you about tonight. The finding of the big idea, the finding of the magic hook, the thing that sets what you are doing apart from everything else. It's called unique selling proposition, but more than that. So you are in for a treat with the remainder of the evening. My good friend John Carlton now has you in his very capable hands. John, thank you very much. Thanks, Dan. Much appreciated. Am I on? Yeah, I'm on. Dan's pretty smart. Someday that boy's going to make some money. Okay, how many people here have heard of me or have any idea who I am? How many of you are mad at me right now? Six or seven in the back, okay? Good, good, good. Okay, how many have no idea who I am and have not even heard at all about me? I need to get an idea where you're at. Okay, you can all leave the room now. What I'm going to do, I'm up here, I am not going to give you guys theory. Most of you have been to events before, right? Two, three-day events and you go home. What's the first thing that happens in the week after you go to an event? What's the first thing that happens? You forget everything you learn, right? Because a lot of it's theory and stuff. So I want to make sure that you cement a lot of this stuff in. So I'm going to give you some exercises. I'll get to that in just a second. Let me find my clicker. Now, Dan spoke briefly about salesmanship. I will explain to you in just a minute who I am and how I got to this point of being on the stage here speaking and why I have a little bit of credibility in this field. The first I want to ask, how many people would like to learn how to sell without selling in their business? Okay, grow up. Not going to happen. Classic salesmanship is the stuff that started working back when the first caveman traded up to a cave with a better view in exchange for a slab of masted on beef to a salesman who knew how to sell. The stuff doesn't change much. I call it classic salesmanship and it has nothing to do with hype. It has nothing to do with being overbearing. You don't need to wear the plaid jacket of the used car salesman. You can be who you are. How many people have an unethical product that they're selling that they're ashamed of? Right, good. A couple of people in the back, the smart ones. The thing is, a lot of people feel shame about salesmanship. They do associate it with the used car salesman with the plaid jacket and all of that. So how many people are uneasy about asking somebody to buy what you have? Be honest. I don't believe any of it. Most of you are a little hesitant to do it or you wouldn't be in the seminar. You'd be relaxing on a beach somewhere. You'd be filthy rich. The thing is, if you have an ethical good product that fixes the problems that your prospect has, then shame on you if you don't do everything you can in your power to make sure that he understands that what you have is what he needs, that you're the person he needs to deal with and he needs to do it when? Right now. That's where classic salesmanship comes in. So what I'm going to do, excuse me. Well, first I'm going to take you through this real quick because usually this is a 90-minute talk I give and I got it down to about an hour. So who that is, because John Carleton, why should you listen to anything? I have to say, my story is interesting in that I started out as a clueless slacker, one of the original clueless slackers. I was 32 years old before I figured out what was going on. I'd been broke for most of my adult life until I figured this stuff out. I got fired from every normal job I ever had. That's serious, including the one mowing the lawn for the next door neighbor. And I was a slacker before they even had the word slacker. The ultimate wake-up call for me was that I lost my job, my girlfriend, and my place to live all within a two-month period. That was when I was about 32, I think. I wound up living in my what? In my car for about six months. One day I woke up on a friend's couch down in San Diego and it dawned on me that if my life was going to change, I was the guy who was going to have to affect that change. Nobody was going to help me, there was going to be no magic. I'd given it 32 years waiting for the magic. Who's this guy? Oh, photographer. I'm just thinking, I missed something, there's a parade coming through. For the next 30 years, I made every mistake possible. I became an advertising expert before I clearly understood what being an advertising expert entailed, but I figured it out really fast and I needed to get as hip as possible, as fast as possible, so I took a speed reading course all within this year of making this realization that I needed to get busy. I took a speed reading course, went to the Torrance Municipal Library and read everything in the Dewey Decimal System from I think 600 to 820, which includes sales, marketing, writing, business, all of that stuff, and just plowed through the entire decks of the library. I did that because I was going to be a freelance writer and I was going to go out into the world of Los Angeles advertising agencies and I figured I needed that education to bring me up to speed with what everybody else was doing. You know what it actually did? It put me light years ahead of the first agency owner I met. These guys were clueless, even more clueless than me, and because when I went through that stack, I found the books that were out of print, like Claude Hopkins, Robert Collier, the books that, I don't know if Dan spelled them out, but these were books that when I later met Gary Halberd, J. Abraham, and a lot of the Joe Shurgerman, and a lot of the very famous guys, and mentored with a lot of them, wrote with them, and partnered with some of them. These were the books that we held in common. Claude Hopkins wrote in the 1920s, Robert Collier in the 50s and 60s. These are books that are out of print and nobody in advertising, even to this day at an agency, pays any attention to the old stuff. They want the new stuff, they want the hot stuff. Me, I went for the old stuff. I started interviewing classic street-wise salesmen. The guys who actually were selling eye to eye, and if they didn't make a sale, they probably weren't going to eat that night. I wanted to learn about that. What I did for the next 30 years is I went out, very proud, because I knew all this stuff, but I still made every mistake possible. I continued to fix things after making the mistakes, and this is the important thing. I took detailed notes along the way. When I decided to step out and be a guru about 12 years ago, I had a deep library of notes that I could tap to be able to teach other people how to do it. I had been breaking this process down all along the way. This is a process based in reality because, again, I made the mistakes, then I corrected the mistakes. This is all real-world stuff. This is an example of a few of the people here. Rich here, I thought Rich was going to be here. Rich Sheffrin, you don't need to read these. These are testimonials from guys, Frank Kern. These are guys who owe a lot of what happened in their life good to the simple tips. A lot of times, just an off-hand comment, or sometimes a full-on consultation. Sometimes they actually studied what I did. This is good. These quadruple stuff, these numbers kind of blur together when you see this stuff, but these are average entrepreneurs who made it sometimes into the upper echelons of their markets. What's more important is what I'm going to teach you today. Notice I put the y'all in there because we're in Nashville. I'm going to teach you about one-tenth of 1% of what I have to teach you, but I'm going to teach you something. I'm going to teach you three drop-dead, simple exercises. Have any of the other speakers made you guys work today? Well, they have. Okay, well, good. Then you'd be used to this. This will actually clear some things up. One is a very quick one. I want to make sure you guys understand what you're really selling. Number two is what I'll talk about for the most of tonight, which is the elevator pitch or your USP. This is the key to everything good that will happen in your business for the rest of your life. Then later in the weekend, I will tell you how to write your first damn good ad in nine minutes. I'm not going to teach you today how to write. How many people are a little afraid of writing, a little fearful of sitting out facing the blank page? Be honest now. Good chunk of the room. How many people have suffered writer's block? Okay, writer's block does not exist. I will tell you right now. Writer's block is just being unprepared when you sit down. A lot of the stuff that Dan was talking about the process you go through, so that when you sit down, you are ready to go. So don't worry about writer's block. Don't worry about any of this. Nothing I'm telling you has anything to do with actually sitting down and writing. By the time you sit down to actually write, after these exercises I do with you, you will have the ad pretty much already done. I'm not going to teach you how to be creative. I think we pretty much squash that during the day. Enough people have talked about this isn't being creative. This is about salesmanship. And of course I'm not going to tell you how to sell from the stage because I suck at it. I'm shy, I'm reserved, I'm an introvert, I'm a vampire, I usually get up around noon. In fact, what time is it now? Tomorrow I'm speaking at 8 o'clock. That's 5 a.m. my time. That's when I usually go to bed. I may have to stay up all night to be able to make it tomorrow. Anyway, exercise number one. Real quick, we have the mics. Usually I have runners running around but we're under a little bit of a time crunch. So if any of you want to talk, there are mics there and there. And the mics are just so they catch it on the film but I don't care if you shout it out but we will talk about it. But basically, anybody, what are you selling? Just go ahead and either step up to the mic if you're close to it or shout it from where you're at. What are you actually selling? Anybody? Experience, he says. Anybody else? Solutions to problems. Relationships. Return on investment. Transformation. How much does transformation go for these days? A pound? How much do relationships go for these days? Anybody else? Entertainment, more money. Guys, it's not the thing that you're selling. It's the result. You're not selling relationships. You're selling a better life. You're not selling more money. You're selling a better life. Goes back to the faster, easier, cheaper, simpler. So a lot of people get confused about what it is you're actually selling. And if you go into writing and ad thinking you're going to tell people how to have a relationship, that ain't going to work because that's not what they want. That's how they may verbalize it. What they really want is a better life that they imagine at that point comes with having a better relationship. Does that make sense if I lost anybody? This is it. This is the first lesson. This is what a lot of people pay me thousands of dollars to sit down and get harangued about. I like to make people cry when I do consultations. Then I know I've broken through and gotten to the essence of this. So a lot of people get very invested in what they're selling. They think they're selling a five-part course in a series of books, in a seminar, and all this stuff. No, not at all. It all boils down to some very basic stuff. What are the results in your prospect's life? How has his life changed? It's the benefits. Okay, this one I'm going to actually have you work at. How many people have heard of the elevator chat? Okay, there's a lot of different versions of it. I use it because here's how you have to think about your USP. First of all, most people know USP from the Rosser Reeves definition, which was a 1950s ad guy in Madison Avenue who wrote slogans for like soap and beer and stuff like that. So they had slogans that went up, cigarettes. I can't even think of one. David, do we have? Yeah, I'd rather fight than switch. Things like that. So the original USP was called the Unique Selling Proposition. He tried to differentiate a cigarette from another cigarette. Most of that stuff isn't going to be relevant for you guys who are doing direct response, but the concept is still the same. So the way I use USP is I talk about positioning yourself in the market uniquely in order to sell. Positioning yourself in your market uniquely in order to sell. It's different. It's not a slogan. It's positioning yourself. You can position yourself so that you may be listened to, you may be respected, but you won't sell at all. You may position yourself in a way where you're just like everybody else. You're just one of the choices out there. You're not standing out. So you want to keep all of this in mind. The elevator chat comes in when, imagine you're on the sixth floor, let's say this place had six floors. You're on the sixth floor. You get on an elevator and you're not sure if the person standing next to you is a prospect or not, but just assume they are. And they turned to you and they say, what do you do? And the doors close and the elevator comes down. Can you describe who you are and why you're important if they were a hot, would be a hot prospect, if they only knew what you had and who you were and why it was so important. Can you do it in six floors? Most marketers can't. And I'm going to change that today. Here's the elevator chat template. We help blank, do blank, even if blank. That's it. Good night. Thanks a lot. Basically, I want you to get this down. We help this group of people do this benefit. You guys know the difference between a feature and a benefit, right? Feature would be the heft, weight, size of the thing. The benefit is what that stuff does for you with the emotional, physical, financial results of it is. Better, cheaper, faster, easier, some kind of quantifier that makes you unique. Even if, and this is important, the worst case believable scenario. Here's a couple of examples. I'm going to have you write your own. Usually when I have plenty of time and I do this and I've given this talk all over the world, is we hand out beer for the winning stuff. And I wound up signing the beer every once in a while, the bottles of beer, and then I found out they were being sold on eBay. And so I, we're not giving out beer tonight. I think I'd go to jail in Nashville if I did that. Anyway, here's an example. I help small business owners and entrepreneurs create their first killer conversations that bring in the big bucks, even if they flunk the English or scared to death of writing. That's kind of like what I do. We help golfers with huge handicaps to immediately start hitting long, straight, gorgeous tee shots and score solo. They win all the money from their buddies, even if you've been slicing, hooking, sculling, hacking and putting like a baboon your entire life. We're on the fifth floor. We're still going down. What have I established at this point? Just about everything that's going to bring that guy, if this is what fulfills his needs in life, he's already interested, okay? This was for a, I believe, an accountant. We help understaffed biz owners, grossing one to 10 million a year, very specific, to pay the least amount of taxes legally owed each year without adding a minute of extra work to your day, even if your bookkeeping is a total disaster area right now. It's an accountant. We help broke people buried in debt to get out of debt within a short time and have more money in your pocket without taking on another job or borrowing. Works even if you have no understanding of finances or economics whatsoever. I think this is the last one. This is for a weight loss thing. We help overweight people to lose 10 to 20 pounds of body fat within one month without ever feeling hungry or deprived or sweaty from exercise. Works even if you failed with every diet you've ever tried. I think, yes. So, I'm going to give you just a couple of minutes and then I want to hear some of these. So, let's do this now. Do it fast. It's okay to make mistakes, but if you don't do this, you won't get it down. So, start now. Are you done? What? We're not there yet. Okay. So, I'm going to give you a quick break. Everybody does it in the room. It's like, okay, door's closed. And we have to have them tell us what their elevator speech is. 90% of even experienced marketers fail. Oh, great ominous space music. That's an excellent first draft. Way to get going, but you haven't really nailed it. You haven't really got down to the essence of this. A lot of you people, when Dan talked about doing two drafts, what he meant was do a ton of prep and then do a draft and then do a brush up draft and kick it out the door. I do do a lot of drafts when I do things. When I work on this, first of all, I don't have a certain number of drafts. I do. I do it until it's right and you know it's right when it can be explained to somebody, a stranger as you go down the elevator, for example, or in a very short period of time. But if this is my target market, they're like, yeah, I get it. For anyone who's afraid of lawyers or anyone who thinks that lawyers are out to get them or screw them just for the money or don't care about them, something along that line. Infomarketingsystem.com helps author, speakers, coaches, and entrepreneurs install an online sales vortex in their business that sucks in prospects and automatically converts anonymous website visitors into lifelong customers and evangelists so you can eliminate all your technical challenges, make more money, and work less even if you don't... Ding, ding, ding, ding, ding, ding, ding. Not bad. Better to overwrite and work your way back than to try to nail it on the first trip, but I lost you halfway through. I got the idea. You helped me with a website and blah, blah, blah. You don't care about the guy who's not a prospect in the elevator. If he's not a prospect, you don't care. If he is a prospect, think about, you guys know there's between hot and warm and cold prospects, right? Hot prospects are ready to go. As soon as they find out you got a solution, checks out, and their cash exchange is handed to him. The cold prospect is still in the market, but needs to be convinced. So run it through all of that. Yours might work with a hot one, but with a cold one, he's going to get turned off. In the grand world of helping people with websites and stuff, you sound kind of generic. There ain't nothing special. Does that make sense? But good first effort. Excellent. Okay, back over here. We help hobbyists with video cameras. Wait, wait, back up. We help. We help hobbyists with video cameras. Edit video that communicates powerfully, even if they can't even spell iMovie. Are you just working with Max? No. It's generic. Real generic. I think what are the problems, for example, and this can help everybody. The micro mimics the macro. When you help one person with something, everybody should be able to get the idea of this. The idea is to go after people who want to be able to edit video that actually makes people feel something. They can start out. Do they have any money? Are you making money at this? Yes. Oh, good. Well, actually not yet. Not yet. Okay, you need to define your market. You need to think of who that guy is, what he's doing now. And I don't know if anybody talked about target markets. And marketers joke around what you really want is stupid people, slow learners with lots of money. That's a perfect market. Very few of those exist, but they do exist. I mean, what is the average restaurant? What does the average restaurant owner do halfway through his first day after the restaurant's open, bright and shining, people have come in and ordered and things are happening? What does he want to do next? Sell the business and retire. It's the same for a doctor. Doctor goes through how many years of medical school to get his license. Haines is shingle, sees his first patient. Next thing, he wants to retire. He's done. So when you, it's some doctors over here, yeah. So when you're talking about restaurant owners, you're not in their head yet. So that's what you need to do when you think about positioning yourself uniquely in this crowded market. How do you do that? And with restaurant owners and everything else, you better understand what they are. So they are probably fried. They've been burned on multiple things. They're probably still buying yellow-page ads that aren't working. They don't understand Yelp. All of these things, they feel lost, neglected, burned. So when you get that stuff, start making notes. Who's been burned? How have they been burned? What are the pain points that they have? What are the fear that they have? What are the two things that most ads work on to sell? Pain. Fear and greed. Who said that? One of my former students, I hope. Fear and greed. The big funnel of marketing just kind of goes just right down to that. So you start to, they're either afraid of something or they're really looking to make a lot of money. And these are good people, bad people. It doesn't matter. That's business. That's capitalism. Restaurant owners, it sounds to me like you haven't spent a lot of time with them. Or if you have, you, like a lot of restaurants, are just numb to what's been going on because it's a devastating business to be in if it's not working. And the worst thing about restaurants, of course, is that you can be working one month and then die off the next month and you don't even know why. So it's a, it's a horrific business to be in. And it's the number one business that people start and the number one to go broke after what? Was it two years? The average restaurant last? Is that right? Yeah. Because that makes sense. So you have a lot, I'm pointing out ways you can make yourself unique. In the even F part, even F, you've been burned. Oh, even if they've been burned by 50% off coupons, something like that. You don't want to get sued. And if you do, don't come back to me. Because I, was I going this way? I think, yeah. We show business owners with employees how to run their business and get the results they've always dreamed of, even if they see themselves as a slave to their business, frustrated and don't want their business to grow anymore because employees are a pain in the ass. Okay. How many people like that? How many people think he's missing the boat? Okay. By the way, I was talking with my pal David Deutsch. David, where you at? Stand up. David Deutsch is an A list writer. He's had the record for the most controls for boardroom.com. He and I have known each other for a long time. He was actually at those events that Dan spoke at down in Key West and he's here as my road dog. So I brought him along. I forgot what I called on you though. So say hello to David. Oh, well I was talking with him about the aggregate. What was that show where you could call a friend or ask the audience? Who wants to be a millionaire? And as David was pointing out, even when it's studied scientifically, aggregate tends to be more right than any single expert talking. When you take the aggregate. There was like Nate Silver in the election yesterday with the New York Times statistician. Was derided by all the pundits. But he was like Billy Bean for Moneyball. He was right on the money. He used numbers and understood emotion. So asking a lot of people, like in an event like this, if you guys, how many people went to lunch with somebody today that they didn't know? Not enough. You should all go to lunch with people you don't know. One of the main things you do at these places is network. And you want to take advantage of the fact that you all are on the same page you're trying to learn and do stuff. And I think that talking to other people and getting their take on this, because everybody here is in your target market more or less, right? There's a lot of information marketers here. Unless your market is more specific than you let on to. Yeah, my market is, and I'm not sure. I'd love to see hands on this. How many of you have more than four employees? Okay. So that's my target. I don't want people with one employee. They don't have a big enough pay in the butt. In the elevator, you say. I help businesses with more than, you know. Perfect. Thanks. Tell me more. No, that's basically it. That's how you start to say it. Because when you talk to more people about this stuff and start with this kind of easy, simple template. By the way, folks, can anybody not see how this can be a headline? Really easy. This is your first headline. I have written headlines like this within five minutes of meeting a client that are still running 20 years later. I'm still getting a royalty check in the mail every month. So I know how to talk these guys. The first headline I had was how to humiliate a black belt making putty in your hands. And that was the app. And I was appealing to a lot of those guys who read and we ran at black belt and things like that. They're kind of looky-loose, but they want to come in that way. It's the same in golf. And I did my first, how many people know my famous golf ad that's still online that's called the Amazing Secrets of a One-Legged Golfer that will help you slash strokes and hit longer to your eyes while it changes a bit? The idea was they had a client who actually learned this from a one-legged golfer. But what we did is we ran three and four-page ads in golf digest and golf magazine, the biggest golf magazines in the biz back in the 90s. And the editors at the magazine mocked us. They said, really? You don't want to take your money. This is not going to work. And it was solid copy for three pages, the first one. It was a right-hand page, double truck ad. Later we added one little photo, but that was a solid copy. They said, this is not going to work. You guys are just wasting your money. These were $70,000 ad outlays. And by the time we went back for the fifth insertion, they tried things like moving it to the back of the mag. They tried to hide it and stuff. And they were embarrassed by it because they didn't understand what we understood and what I understood, but I knew this. How many people golf? The average golfer can't do what to save his life. Break 100. So we started running ads. The next one we ran was, how does this overweight golfer crippled by arthritis and unable to do a complete backswing still humiliate PGA pros on a regular basis in the underground golfing world? This is the guy who went around and just, you know, humiliated. I like the word humiliate back. I was using it quite a bit, but I was speaking to the average reader, the guy who didn't think Tiger Woods could teach him anything. A lot of people make the mistake you make of thinking online is a market. It's not. Online is just a, another description of a medium for marketers to be in. So who in the online world is going to benefit the most? And you start small. Another tactic a lot of you are, are misusing is you're trying to sell three, four things at once. If you're having any trouble at all, you're stumbling or you had to sell one thing. You can always sell more things to people who become clients or customers or sign up on your list. So sell one thing. So you can sell one thing to pick any substratum of the market and just try that out and target, especially if you're using, using AdWords, you're, you're pulling, not pushing. I, I, I work with rookie crowds a lot. You guys are obviously not rookie. So you guys were really tough to do, but sometimes I get people that don't get this. So kudos to you. Now, I just want to remind you how deals get closed. This is a free lesson. It's very short. It takes seconds. What you want your customer to come away, your, your prospect to come away with it from your first conversation with him is rational reasons and lizard brain reasons. Real quick, the rational reasons is what they will tell their wife when they buy, what they'll tell their neighbor who always tries to make them feel bad, how they will justify it to their drinking buddies who think they're an idiot. Those are the rational reasons. I bought the Porsche because it's a great investment in a vehicle, one of the finest machines made. And then there are the lizard brain reasons and you have to get to that too. You have to support them. Why does a guy buy a Porsche? Yeah, we don't even, you know, yeah, of course. Midlife crisis number 12, you know. But you have to tap into that. It's greed and fear and a sense of power and a sense of all kinds of things, but you have to approach both. Okay? It's all about grabbing their attention amidst the chaos, unique. The unique part of positioning yourself. Learning them into your world because you can convince somebody, you know, it's easy to convince someone to say, that's a great product you got there. That's really good. I could probably use it. I think sometime down the road, I just may buy that. That's what we call getting them up on the fence. Professionals knock them off the fence. So you lure them into your world and you help them realize that this is what they need and want and you are the person they want to deal with. That pretty much is the essence of old school classic salesmanship.
